coredata.dc:title: Polyvinyl alcohol/cellulose nanocrystals incorporated with apple waste-derived carbon dots as an excellent UV food packaging material
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pham, Thi Lien
Nguyen, Van Son
coredata.dc:description: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation has significantly reduced food quality. With the increase in UV radiation to Earth due to ozone layer depletion, the need for eco-friendly UV-blocking food packaging materials is becoming very important. This work synthesized carbon dots (CD) from apple waste and then incorporated them into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)/cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) to develop UV-blocking packaging materials. The CD was even distributed and highly compatible with the PVA/CNC matrix (5 wt% of CNC) to form a compact and continuous film without defects. In particular, the UV-blocking ability of PVA/CNC/CD was substantially improved. PVA/CNC/CD 1 % (1 wt% of CD) exhibits excellent UV-blocking ability, which can block about 78.2 % of UVA and 91 % of UVB. The UV protection factor of this composite was 5.9 times higher than that of PVA/CNC. In addition, the prepared composite films keep high transparency with over 70 % transmittance of visible light at 660 nm. The potential use of developed composites as UV-blocking packaging materials was demonstrated by lab-scale packaging tests with cherry tomatoes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Apple waste
Cellulose nanocrystals
Active packaging
UV absorbent

UV-blocking ability of composite film for food packaging
Packaging test of cherry tomatoes under UV irradiation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) lattice fringe, (c) particle size distribution, and (d) the ATR-FTIR spectrum of CD.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD spectra of PVA/CNC/CD composites.
SEM images of (a) PVA/CNC, (b) PVA/CNC/CD 0.25 %, (c) PVA/CNC/CD 0.5 %, and (d) PVA/CNC/CD 1 % composites.
(a) UV-Vis transmittance, (b) the mean of UVA and UVB transmittance, (c) UPF, and (d) the transmittance at 660 nm of PVA/CNC/CD composites.
Appearance changes of cherry tomatoes under UV irradiation for 15 days.
Weight loss of cherry tomatoes according to UV irradiation times.
Detailed composition of the fabricated composites.
UV-barrier properties of previously reported PVA-based composite films.

head.ce:title: Polyvinyl alcohol/cellulose nanocrystals incorporated with apple waste-derived carbon dots as an excellent UV food packaging material

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Science, Vietnam Academy of Science and Technology, 18 Hoang Quoc Viet, Cau Giay, Hanoi, Vietnam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials Science, Vietnam Academy of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Materials Science, Vietnam Academy of Science and Technology, 18 Hoang Quoc Viet, Cau Giay, Hanoi, Vietnam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Technology Center, Le Quy Don Technical University, Hanoi, Vietnam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Technology Center, Le Quy Don Technical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Technology Center, Le Quy Don Technical University, Hanoi, Vietnam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation has significantly reduced food quality. With the increase in UV radiation to Earth due to ozone layer depletion, the need for eco-friendly UV-blocking food packaging materials is becoming very important. This work synthesized carbon dots (CD) from apple waste and then incorporated them into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)/cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) to develop UV-blocking packaging materials. The CD was even distributed and highly compatible with the PVA/CNC matrix (5 wt% of CNC) to form a compact and continuous film without defects. In particular, the UV-blocking ability of PVA/CNC/CD was substantially improved. PVA/CNC/CD 1 % (1 wt% of CD) exhibits excellent UV-blocking ability, which can block about 78.2 % of UVA and 91 % of UVB. The UV protection factor of this composite was 5.9 times higher than that of PVA/CNC. In addition, the prepared composite films keep high transparency with over 70 % transmittance of visible light at 660 nm. The potential use of developed composites as UV-blocking packaging materials was demonstrated by lab-scale packaging tests with cherry tomatoes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Preserving and extending the shelf life of food is becoming an urgent problem due to rapid population growth and depletion of agricultural resources [1]. Exposuring light, particularly ultraviolet (UV) light, is one of the main reasons for faster spoilage of food. Although only 5 % of the Sun’s light is UV, its energy is sufficient to affect the quality and shelf life of packaging foods [1]. UV light not only changes the appearance, color, and weight of food but also degrades food compositions (accelerating lipid oxidation, photodegradation of bioactive compounds, and breaking protein structures) [1,2].

To against the effect of UV light on packaged food, packaging material with high UV-blocking ability has been developed [3]. In this packaging material, UV absorbent agents are used to provide UV-blocking ability. Various works have developed UV-blocking agents for active packaging such as nanomaterials (TiO2, ZnO) or natural-derived active agents (flavonoids, lignin, polyphenolic compounds, and essential oils) [1]. Although many active ingredients have been proposed, they still have some limitations such as being expensive, relatively toxic, and less efficient. Therefore, finding a novel UV-blocking agent that is cost-effective, eco-friendly, and highly efficient is still a critical problem for food packaging.

Carbon dots (CD) are novel candidates for UV blocking agents due to their unique structure (a nano-sized sphere consisting of a carbon core and various functional groups on its surface) [4,5]. Furthermore, carbon dots are not toxic to human health and can be fabricated from cheap and common materials such as agricultural waste. Therefore, carbon dots are a promising candidate as low-cost and eco-friendly UV-blocking agents for food packaging [6].

Apple waste is one of the largest wastes from the juice industry [7]. Fabricating carbon dots from apple waste not only makes a useful material but also solves the concern about environmental issues. PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) is a promising food packaging material due to its advantages (biodegradability, low cost, excellent oxygen barrier, and easy film formation). The incorporation of cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) not only improves the mechanical and water-resistant properties of PVA [8] but also enhances the UV-blocking ability of CD [9]. In addition, PVA/CNC composites exhibited thermoplastic properties and sufficient heat-sealability which are important requirements for food packaging materials [10]. We hypothesize that PVA/CNC/CD nanocomposite could serve as an excellent UV food-packaging material due to the properties and interactions of PVA/CNC, and CD.

In this study, CD is fabricated from apple waste using the hydrothermal process. The prepared CD was characterized by morphology, size distribution, and surface functional groups. The PVA/CNC/CD composites were fabricated by solvent casting. The interactions of CD and PVA/CNC matrix are characterized. Finally, the potential use of PVA/CNC/CD composites as UV-blocking packaging materials was evaluated by UV-Vis analysis and lab-scale packing test on the fresh cherry tomatoes.

PVA (beads;
M
w
85000–124000; 99+% hydrolyzed; Sigma-Aldrich) and CNCs (powder, diameter ranges of 10–20 nm; length ranges of 300–900 nm; 92 % crystallinity; Nanografi) were purchased, respectively. In addition, apple waste was collected from a local recycling bin (Hanoi, Vietnam). All the materials were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparing carbon dot (CD) from apple waste
ce:section.ce:para: The apple waste was washed with deionized (DI) water several times to remove dirt and contaminants, oven-dried (60 °C, 12 h), and ground into fine powders using a kitchen grinder. Apple waste suspension was prepared by adding apple waste powder (4 g) into DI water (100 mL). This suspension was magnetic-stirred at 680 rpm for 30 min. The apple waste suspension was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and then heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After that, the prepared mixture was naturally cooled down (to room temperature) and centrifuged (12,000 rpm for 30 min). The obtained supernatant was filtered using a 0.2 µm filter membrane. Next, the small molecules were removed from the fabricated suspension through dialysis using a dialysis membrane (1 kDa) against DI water for 3 days to obtain the CD aqueous suspension with a concentration of 1.122±0.008 mg/mL. This suspension is stored at 4 °C for further use.

First, PVA beards and DI water were used to prepare a 3 wt% aqueous PVA solution. The solution was heated to 90 °C under continuous magnetic stirring at 400 rpm to form the PVA solution. CNC powder was added to DI water, magnetic stirred (1200 rpm, 3 h), and then sonicated (40 kHz, 200 W, 1 h) to obtain CNC suspension of 1 wt%. From our previous work, we selected PVA/CNC with 5 wt% of CNCs as the matrix for PVA/CNC/CD composites because this composite exhibited the highest mechanical properties [11]. Varying quantities of CD (0–1 wt%) were incorporated into the selected matrix. The detailed composition of PVA/CNC/CD composites is shown in Table 1. The CD suspension was added into PVA/CNC suspension and then magnetic stirred for 3 h. The composite films were fabricated by solvent-casting in Petri dishes of 90 mm. PVA/CNC/CD composite film was naturally dried at room temperature for 3 days before peeling off from Petri dishes. The obtained films were conditioned (25 °C, 50 % RH) for 3 days before analysis. The amount of the solvent mixture was controlled to obtain films with a thickness of about 100 µm.

A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100) operating at 100 kV was used to analyze the morphology, lattice fringe, and particle size distribution of apple waste-derived CD. To prepare the TEM sample, 0.1 mg/mL of CD was dropped on a carbon/copper grid. This grid was dried at room temperature before observation. ImageJ and Origin software were used to analyze the average particle size and distribution of CD.

Functional groups of CD and chemical interaction between CD and PVA/CNC matrix were investigated by Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR, IRAffinity) in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. The spectra were obtained in ranges of 4000–500 cm−1 with 4 cm−1 of resolution. To prepare the CD sample, CD suspension was cast in a glass slide, and then dried in the oven at 60 °C for 24 h to completely remove moisture before measuring.

XRD spectra of CD and fabricated composites were investigated by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X’Pert PRO MPD, Cu Kα radiation with λ = 0.154 nm). The XRD data were obtained in the range of 2θ =10–70°. To prepare the CD sample, CD suspension was cast in a glass slide, and then dried at room temperature. This step was repeated until obtaining a CD film thickness of around 10 µm before measuring.

The surface and cross-sectional images of fabricated composites were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU-70). The samples were cut at different composites. Cross-sectional specimens were prepared by immersing the composite film in liquid nitrogen for 10 min before immediately breaking. All samples were platinum-coated before analyzing. The SEM images were obtained using an acceleration voltage of 5 kV, a working distance of 6 mm, and a magnification of 2000× (for surface image) and 5000× (for cross-sectional image).

The ultraviolet (UV)-visible (Vis) light absorption of prepared composite films was analyzed with an OPTIZEN POP UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Samples (40 × 40 mm2) from different positions of films were prepared. The UV-Vis data were obtained with light wavelengths from 200 to 800 nm with a 1 nm resolution. To evaluate the UV-blocking ability of composites, the UV transmittance (UVA, UVB) and UV-protection factor (UPF) were estimated based on AS/NZS 4399 as follows:
UVA
%
=
∑
1
n
T
i
n
,
UVB
%
=
∑ 1 m
T j
m
,
(1)
UPF =
∑
290
400
E
λ

S
λ

∆
λ

290
400
E
λ

λ

T

×
∆
λ
where Ti, Tj, Eλ, Sλ, Δλ, and Tλ are the spectral transmittance in range of 315–400 nm, the spectral transmittance in range of 290–315 nm, relative erythemal spectral effectiveness (Table B1 in AS/NZS 4399), solar spectral irradiance (Table B2 in AS/NZS 4399), wavelength step (nm), and the spectral transmittance of the sample at wavelength λ (nm), respectively.

Lab-scale tests with fresh cherry tomato models were conducted to present the UV-blocking ability of fabricated composites. Samples of cherry tomatoes (similar size, appearance, and maturity degree) from the local market were selected and then placed in paper cups. One cup was a blank sample without covering. Other cups were covered by PVA/CNC, PVA/CNC/CD 0.25 %, PVA/CNC/CD 0.5 %, and PVA/CNC/CD 1 %, respectively. All paper cups were stored under UV irradiation by 365 nm UV lamps for 15 days at room temperature. The effect of UV light on cherry tomatoes was evaluated by visual examination (changing in appearance of samples) and weight loss. To assess the appearance change, images of cherry tomatoes were captured every day. The composite films were removed before imaging and then covered again. For weight loss, samples were weighed every 3 days for 15 days. The weight loss according to time was determined using the following equation:
(2)
Weight loss
%
=
M
s
−
M
t
M
s
× 100

where M
s and M
t were the sample weight (g) at the starting time and day t (0, 3, 6, 9, 12, and 15) of the storage process, respectively.

The morphology, lattice fringe, and particle size distribution of the prepared CD were investigated by TEM as shown in Fig. 1a-c. The fabricated CDs presented nearly quasi-spherical shapes with diameters ranging from ∼2.2–8.3 nm (average diameter of about 5.1 nm). In addition, the monodisperse of CD particles can be seen in Fig. 1a because of the electrostatic repulsion of functional groups on their surfaces. The high-resolution-TEM image of CD with the lattice fringe is presented in Fig. 1b. The estimated lattice spacing of CD is about 0.34 nm, which is similar to the (002) lattice spacing of graphite [9]. The presence of graphite-like structures in CDs suggests the existence of conjugated π electron domains. These aspects confirmed the successfully fabricated CDs from apple waste.

As shown in Fig. 1d, the existing functional groups of CD’s surface were further investigated by ATR-FTIR analysis. Stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (-OH) and/or amine (-NH2) groups corresponded to strong absorption peaks around 3255 cm−1 [12]. A 1567 cm−1 peak was due to the vibration and deformation of N-H bonds [13]. The peak at 1397 cm−1 corresponded to the C-NH-C groups [14]. The plane bending of -OH bonds caused the peak absorption at 660 cm−1 [12]. Moreover, other peaks at 1700 and 1033 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of CO and C-O-C bonds, respectively [15,16]. Based on this FTIR result, the presence of many functional groups (-OH, CO, -COOH, -NH2) on CD’s surface could be confirmed.

The XRD spectrum of CD is presented in Fig. S1. The XRD pattern of CD presented a semi-crystalline structure. The strong peak around 25 °C corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of the graphite structure [9]. The estimated lattice spacing of CD from XRD data according to Bragg's law is about 0.36 nm, which is consistent with TEM observations. This lattice spacing of the synthesized CDs is slightly larger than that of graphite (0.33 nm) which may be a result of the existence of functional groups on the CD surface (-OH, CO, -COOH, NH2) as confirmed by FTIR analysis. The presence of these functional groups could prevent the close packing of carbon atoms in the graphite crystal lattice, resulting in larger inter-lattice spacing [9].

The PVA/CNC spectra exhibited the typical peaks of PVA and CNCs (Fig. S2). The stretching vibration and plane bending of -OH groups in PVA caused strong peaks at 3278 and 1560 cm−1, respectively [17]. The vibration of C-O-C groups in CNCs corresponded to a weak peak at 1052 cm−1 [18]. Compared to PVA/CNC, although no new peaks are occurring in PVA/CNC/CD spectra, some changes could be observed ( Fig. 2a). Compared to PVA/CNC, the peaks around 3278 cm−1 of PVA/CNC/CD composites appeared broader and stronger. In addition, these peaks shifted slightly toward lower frequencies as the CD content increased. Moreover, the absorption peak at 1560 cm−1 was decreased with increasing CD content as shown in Fig. S3. These aspects suggested that more hydrogen bonds have been formed due to the interaction of hydroxyl groups of PVA/CNC with functional groups of CD [4].

The XRD spectra of fabricated composites are shown in Fig. 2b. The PVA/CNC exhibited a semi-crystalline structure with peaks at 19.7° (corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of PVA) and 22.5° (corresponding to the (200) crystal plane of PVA and the (200) crystal plane of CNCs). In general, no obvious changes could be observed when comparing the XRD spectra of PVA/CNC and PVA/CNC/CD composites. The PVA/CNC/CD composites still exhibited a semi-crystalline structure with diffraction peaks similar to PVA/CNC. This observation could explained by low CD content in PVA/CNC/CD composites [4].

Fig. 3 presents the surface and cross-sectional images of fabricated composites. PVA/CNC surface exhibits a smooth, compact surface without any defects (cracks, holes) indicating the high compatibility between components (PVA, CNCs). PVA/CNC/CD presented a similar surface with slightly rougher compared to PVA/CNC. The surface roughness of the composites appeared to increase as the CD content increased. However, there are not any defects on the composite surface. Moreover, cross-sectional images of the PVA/CNC/CD composites were homogeneous without CD agglomeration into large clusters. These observations suggest a uniform distribution of CD in the PVA/CNC matrix and good compatibility among components of prepared composites. This result can be explained by hydrogen bonds that can be formed between the components of composites as a result of FTIR analysis and the low content of CD in PVA/CNC/CD composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-blocking ability of composite film for food packaging
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4, the PVA/CNC film presented poor UV-blocking ability with 71.97 % UVA and 69.27 % UVB transmitted through the composite film. The UPF (ultraviolet protection factor) of PVA/CNC is quite low at only 1.43. The UV-blocking ability of PVA/CNC/CD composites was significantly improved with the incorporation of CDs. In particular, PVA/CNC/CD 1 % exhibited the highest UV-barrier property among the fabricated composite materials. Only 21.8 % UVA and 9 % UVB can go through the PVA/CNC/CD 1 % composite film. Moreover, the UPF value of PVA/CNC/CD 1 % was 5.9 times higher than that of neat PVA/CNC. This observation confirmed that the incorporation of CD endows the improvement in the UV-blocking ability of PVA/CNC/CD composites. The mechanism of the UV-blocking ability of PVA/CNC/CD composite is the UV absorption ability of CD. Two main reasons can explain the strong UV absorption ability of CD. Firstly, CD structures are rich in conjugated π-electron systems. These delocalized electrons can readily absorb photons in the UV range leading to strong UV absorption [19]. Secondly, CDs possess functional groups on their surface such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and amino groups. These groups can interact with UV photons and increase the surface area of CDs, resulting in enhancing their UV absorption capabilities [20,21]. Interestingly, while most common UV absorbers such as titanium dioxide, zinc oxide, benzotriazoles, and triazines absorb UV light and convert it into heat, CDs emit most of the absorbed ultraviolet energy as visible light [22]. Therefore, CD-based UV-blocking materials do not significantly increase the temperature under UV radiation. This is a very useful property for UV-blocking food packaging materials.

It is noticed that all PVA/CNC/CD composites exhibited high transparency with light transmission of more than 70 % at 660 nm wavelength (Fig. 4d). The transparency of PVA/CNC/CD composite only decreased by less than 7.5 % compared to that of PVA/CNC.

As shown in Table 2, compared with previously reported PVA-based composite films, these PVA/CNC/CD composite films exhibited high UV blocking ability while maintaining good transparency. It is worth noting that only a small amount of CD extracted from apple waste compared with other active agents could provide similar UV-blocking ability in PVA-based nanocomposite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Packaging test of cherry tomatoes under UV irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the UV-blocking ability of fabricated composites in food packaging, the lab-scale packaging test of cherry tomatoes was conducted. The surface of blank and PVA/CNC cases presented wrinkles after only 1 and 3 days, respectively ( Fig. 5). These wrinkles become more evident as the irradiation time increases. By contrast, PVA/CNC/CD composites keep fresh cherry tomatoes in shape without any noticeable wrinkles from 7 to 15 days.

The weight of fresh cherry tomatoes will decrease when exposed to UV light because the tomato surface heats up, causing water to evaporate. As shown in Fig. 6, the blank samples lost 22.4 wt% of their weights after 15 days of UV irradiation. Samples covered by PVA/CNC film decreased by around 17.1 wt% their weight indicating poor UV-blocking ability of PVA/CNC. It was observed that the weight loss decreased significantly with increasing CD content in PVA/CNC/CD composite films. For PVA/CNC/CD 1 % case, cherry tomatoes decreased only 6.5 wt% of their weight after 15 days.
These results demonstrated the potential use of prepared composites as excellent UV packaging materials.

In this study, CD has been successfully synthesized from apple waste and incorporated into PVA/CNC to fabricate UV-blocking packaging materials. The CD has a quasi-spherical shape with a diameter in ranges of 2.2 – 8.3 nm with multifunctional groups (-OH, -COOH, -NH2) attaching to a carbonaceous core. The incorporation of CD into the PVA/CNC matrix remains high transparency, compact surface, and semi-crystalline structure, but significantly enhances the UV-blocking ability. The UPF of PVA/CNC/CD 1 % is 5.9 times that of PVA/CNC. The packaging test of fresh cherry tomatoes demonstrated the excellent UV-blocking ability of PVA/CNC/CD composites as potential food packaging materials.

Thi Lien Pham: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Van Son Nguyen: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors received no financial support for research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2024.111091.
